Long before time was measured by clocks, the world was an endless emerald green. The Sun, a fiery deity named Solen, drove his chariot across the sky, warming the earth endlessly. Down below, deep in the sprawling forest, lived Auri, the spirit of the ancient oaks. Auri loved Solen from afar, stretching her leafy fingers as high as they could reach to graze his golden rays.

One day, Solen noticed her devotion. Struck by Auri’s quiet strength, he descended, wrapping her in an embrace of pure, blinding light. But Solen’s nature was too fierce. His heat began to scorch her delicate leaves, turning them brilliant shades of crimson, copper, and gold. 

Realizing his touch was destroying the very thing he loved, Solen wept and pulled away, retreating to the far edge of the sky. Stripped of his warmth, Auri’s bright leaves withered and fell to the cold earth, leaving her bare. 

Thus, the cycle was born. Every year, Solen draws near to embrace Auri, painting her canopy in fiery hues of passion. And every year, he must pull away to save her, bringing the chill of winter until she can bloom anew.

Now, whenever the winds howl, the dust swirls and ignites, creating ribbons of green, pink, and violet. Today, mortals call it the aurora borealis, unaware they are witnessing the glowing remnants of the wolves' eternal, tragic battle.
